doc_type: Petition for Writ of Habeas Corpus (Immediate Discharge from Parole)
key_facts: Petitioner: Brendan Ngehsi Newanforbi, CDC No. BF3571, age 29, residing at 1455 Cat Tail Drive, Stockton, CA 95204
Petitioner was convicted on January 18, 2018 in Santa Clara County Superior Court and sentenced to six years in state prison for violations of Penal Code sections 289(a)(1)(A) and 220(a)(1)
Because offenses require registration under Penal Code section 290, Petitioner is subject to a maximum parole term of ten years under Penal Code section 3000(b)(2)(B)
Petitioner was released to parole on December 2, 2020, with a controlling discharge date of December 2, 2030
As of the filing date (March 31, 2026), Petitioner has served over five years on parole with zero revocations, zero new criminal offenses, and zero arrests
CSRA Score: 2 (Low Risk); LS/CMI Score: 5 (Level 2 Below Average Risk) — less than half the regulatory retention threshold of 11 under 15 CCR § 3574(f)(1); Stable-2007 Score: 6 (Moderate)
CDCR conducted a discharge review on December 1, 2025 and denied discharge; Parole Agent Long Moua recommended retain; Unit Supervisor Gary Noguchi and Parole Administrator Keely Dodd concurred

The denial was based on: (1) Policy 19-03 time-served bar (Petitioner had served only ~4 of the required 6.5 years); (2) incomplete treatment (not in maintenance phase); (3) minor technical violations; (4) unemployment
Discharge review was conducted using obsolete CDCR Form 1502-DR rather than the mandated CDCR Form 3043 required by 15 CCR § 3574(f), effective April 17, 2025
No Containment Team Meeting (CTM) was conducted as required by 15 CCR § 3574(c); no treatment provider signature was obtained; no 30-day written notice was delivered to Petitioner
Petitioner holds a valid California CDL and earned $39,553 (2022), $11,145 (2023), and $55,894 (2024) from commercial trucking despite parole conditions suppressing his career

In March 2025, Petitioner received a FedEx Office job offer (Retail Customer Service Associate, $20.50/hr, start April 8, 2025). The position required Modesto training that conflicted with mandatory HOPE Program sessions

Parole Agent A. Derrick and HOPE Program Director Katie Palacios jointly denied the scheduling accommodation request; Petitioner was coerced into resigning from FedEx Office on approximately April 17, 2025
The Department subsequently cited Petitioner's resulting unemployment as a primary aggravating factor (rated '3', most severe negative rating) in the discharge denial

Petitioner's only technical violations in the review period were four minor incidents (missed treatment sessions and GPS charging failures), all resolved with verbal reprimands or PACT referrals — none resulted in custody
A prior habeas petition was filed in April/May 2025 and denied by Judge Geffon on August 27, 2025; that petition raised different claims (three-year vs. ten-year parole term per Abstract of Judgment)
Petitioner filed a Government Tort Claim on February 10, 2026
Petition filed in propria persona on March 31, 2026, directed to the Superior Court of California, County of Santa Clara
Respondents: Keely Dodd CDCR (individual capacity, approved the December 2025 denial) and Jeff Macomber, Secretary of CDCR

dates.event: Conviction in Santa Clara County Superior Court (PC §§ 289(a)(1)(A), 220(a)(1))
Release to parole (controlling discharge date December 2, 2030)
Technical violation — failure to attend sex offender treatment (Verbal Reprimand)
FedEx Office offer letter extended to Petitioner ($20.50/hr, Retail Customer Service Associate)
FedEx Office anticipated start date / training conflict with HOPE Program
15 CCR § 3574 (Sex Offender Treatment regulations) effective date
Petitioner coerced to resign from FedEx Office (approximate date)
Technical violation — failure to attend sex offender treatment (PACT Referral, Verbal Reprimand)
Technical violation — failure to charge GPS device for one full hour (COP Verbal Reprimand)
Technical violation — failure to properly charge GPS device (PACT Referral, Verbal Reprimand)
Prior habeas petition denied by Judge Geffon
CDCR Discharge Review Report (Form 1502-DR) — discharge denied
Five-year parole supervision milestone (basis for discharge eligibility argument)
Petition for Writ of Habeas Corpus filed
In re Dwyer order (San Mateo Super. Ct. No. SC-080355A/HC-3020) — precedent cited

arguments: CDCR Policy 19-03 exceeds statutory authority: Penal Code § 3008(d)(2) uses 'up to' the entire parole period, creating a discretionary range; Policy 19-03's mandatory 6.5-year minimum converts that discretion into a categorical bar, rendering the statutory language surplusage (People v. Rizo; In re Gadlin (2020) 10 Cal.5th 915)

Policy 19-03 is an invalid 'underground regulation' because it imposes a binding rule of general application (mandatory minimum supervision duration) without being adopted through the APA notice-and-comment process; it is therefore void under Tidewater Marine Western, Inc. v. Bradshaw (1996) 14 Cal.4th 557

The 'incomplete treatment' denial ground violates 15 CCR § 3574(d) (effective April 17, 2025), which explicitly states supervised persons may complete a sex offender management program before discharge, and § 3574(e), which decouples treatment status from parole discharge eligibility

CDCR's use of the obsolete Form 1502-DR instead of the mandated Form 3043 violates 15 CCR § 3574(f) and deprived Petitioner of the objective actuarial thresholds that would have demonstrated ineligibility for retention; the form substituted subjective narrative criteria for objective numerical thresholds

The denial violates substantive due process: under In re Lawrence (2008) 44 Cal.4th 1181 and In re Taylor (2015) 60 Cal.4th 1019, parole decisions must be based on 'some evidence' of current dangerousness with a rational nexus to individual circumstances; the denial contains no individualized finding of current public safety risk despite Petitioner's validated low-risk scores

The denial violates procedural due process: no advance notice, no opportunity to be heard, no neutral decision-maker, no CTM conducted per § 3574(c), no written good-cause finding on Form 3043, no treatment provider signature, no 30-day written notice (Morrissey v. Brewer; Board of Pardons v. Allen; Accardi doctrine)

The circular unemployment rationale constitutes arbitrary and capricious state action: CDCR's own parole conditions destroyed Petitioner's commercial trucking career and forced his resignation from FedEx; the Department then weaponized the resulting unemployment as the primary aggravating factor, despite unemployment not being a statutory basis for retention under Penal Code § 3001

When at least one ground of an administrative decision is improper, the entire decision must be vacated (California Highway Patrol v. Superior Court (2008) 158 Cal.App.4th 726); here at least two grounds (time bar, incomplete treatment standard) are demonstrably unlawful

Exhaustion of administrative remedies is excused on three independent grounds: futility (policies are fixed and absolute as testified under oath in In re Dwyer), pure question of law (statutory interpretation not requiring agency fact-finding), and inadequacy (Form 602 process cannot invalidate regulations or declare policies ultra vires)

Continued supervision serves no legitimate public safety purpose and becomes punitive rather than protective; Petitioner's circumstances mirror those of Frederick Dwyer, for whom the San Mateo Superior Court exercised § 3008(d)(2) authority to terminate program participation
evidence_cited: CDCR Discharge Review Report (Form 1502-DR), dated December 1, 2025 (Exhibit A)

In re Dwyer, San Mateo Super. Ct. Case No. SC-080355A/HC-3020, Order dated March 20, 2023 — CDCR officials testified under oath that agency policy prohibits completion of sex offender management programs prior to discharge (Exhibit B)
15 Cal. Code Regs. § 3574 (Sex Offender Treatment regulations, effective April 17, 2025) including CDCR Form 3043 (Exhibit C)
Centerline Wage Reference (Exhibit D) — documenting CDL earning capacity (~$72,000/year)
FedEx Office employment offer letter (March 25, 2025, $20.50/hr, Stockton location) and related email correspondence from Danielle Sutton and Iesha Celestine (Exhibit E)
Alliance for Constitutional Sex Offense Laws v. Macomber, Sacramento Super. Ct. Case No. 23STCP02378 (Exhibit F)
Petitioner's CSRA Score of 2 (Low Risk) and LS/CMI Score of 5 (Below Average Risk / Level 2), both far below the § 3574(f)(1) retention threshold of 11
Stable-2007 Score of 6 (Moderate) — at threshold of 4 or higher for retention
Federal W-2 wage statements confirming employment with at least six commercial carriers (2022–2024): earnings of $39,553 (2022), $11,145 (2023), $55,894 (2024)

Unit Supervisor Gary Noguchi's own comments in the Discharge Review Report conceding Petitioner 'should be commended for maintaining compliance with parole supervision' and has 'no restitution obligations'
Treatment compliance records: active participation in HOPE provider sex offender treatment, Core Phase (Block 1, Module 4 of 7); Victim Empathy homework presented; relapse prevention plan developed
Record of approximately seven clinician changes in five years and provider's admitted loss of completed homework
procedural_significance: This petition is a direct challenge to a December 1, 2025 administrative discharge denial, distinct from a prior habeas petition denied August 27, 2025 (which raised different claims about the length of the parole term). The case is significant on multiple fronts: it tests the validity of CDCR Policy 19-03 as a potential 'underground regulation' under the APA; it asserts the newly adopted 15 CCR § 3574 (effective April 17, 2025) as binding authority that the Department itself violated eight months after enactment; it challenges the systemic practice — confirmed under oath in In re Dwyer — of treating sex offender treatment completion as structurally impossible before parole discharge; and it raises a novel 'state-induced unemployment as pretext' theory that implicates the same HOPE Program Director Katie Palacios and Parole Agent A. Derrick who appear as defendants in related civil matter Newanforbi v. Palacios et al. (STK-CV-UF-2026-0001032). The petition is filed in Santa Clara County Superior Court (the committing court) and seeks immediate discharge effective December 2, 2025, a declaration that Policy 19-03 is ultra vires, and a declaration that use of the obsolete Form 1502-DR renders the December 2025 determination void. The Government Tort Claim filed February 10, 2026 preserves related damages claims.
